label: ai
topic: The competitive pressure at an elite university causes students to lose their personal identity
text:
There is a very specific type of smile you learn to perfect during your first semester at this university. It is bright, wide, and entirely hollow. You deploy it on Locust Walk when an acquaintance asks how your week is going. "So good!" you beam, tightening your grip on your third iced coffee of the day. "Just really busy, you know?" You say this despite having slept four hours, bombed a macroeconomics midterm, and seriously questioned whether you belong at this school at all. This is the phenomenon we call "Penn Face"—the unspoken, campus-wide agreement to project an image of effortless perfection while silently drowning under the immense pressure of elite academic life.

I wore that mask so tightly during my sophomore year that I almost forgot what my actual face looked like. I had bought completely into the myth that success here meant maintaining an overloaded course schedule, securing executive board positions in three different competitive consulting clubs, and projecting a vibrant social life on Instagram, all without breaking a single sweat. When I looked around the lecture halls, everyone else seemed to be managing it flawlessly. They were landing prestigious summer internships in November, running marathons, and speaking multiple languages, all while appearing impossibly well-rested. I convinced myself that I was the only one struggling, the admissions mistake who had somehow slipped through the cracks and was now desperately trying to keep up the charade.

The tragedy of Penn Face is not just the physical exhaustion it masks; it is the profound loss of identity it demands. In my desperate attempt to keep pace with the relentless achievements of my peers, I began to measure my human worth entirely by my resume. I stopped reading books for pleasure. I stopped calling my parents because I felt I had no impressive updates to share with them. Every hour spent not studying, networking, or optimizing my LinkedIn profile felt like a moral failure. I became a machine calibrated solely for academic and professional achievement, but I lost touch with the passions, quirks, and simple joys that made me who I was before I arrived on this campus. I was burning out, yet I completely refused to ask for help because admitting I was overwhelmed felt like admitting defeat in a silent, high-stakes competition.

The breaking point didn't come with a dramatic, cinematic collapse, but rather a quiet, overwhelming emptiness. I remember sitting in the library stacks at two in the morning, staring at a blank Word document, and realizing I simply couldn't write another sentence. The tears came, quietly at first, and then uncontrollably. In that moment of complete depletion, the sheer absurdity of the facade became painfully clear to me. What was the point of pretending to be a superhuman machine if it left me feeling completely hollow inside?

The next day, when a friend asked me how I was doing, I took a deep breath and broke the cardinal rule of campus culture. "I'm actually doing terribly," I said. "I'm exhausted, I'm behind in all my classes, and I feel really lonely." What happened next was revelatory. She didn't judge me. She didn't look at me with pity. Instead, her own polished smile cracked, her shoulders dropped, and she exhaled a long breath. "Me too," she confessed. "I literally cry in the shower almost every day."

That single moment of vulnerability did more for my mental health than any university-sponsored wellness seminar ever could. We desperately need to dismantle the culture of effortless perfection at elite institutions. The prestige of our university should never demand the sacrifice of our sanity. We have to start valuing authenticity over optics. This means having the courage to admit when we are overwhelmed, to normalize dropping a class when it becomes too much to handle, and to recognize that failure is not an indictment of our character but a natural, necessary part of learning. Breaking Penn Face requires a radical kind of bravery. It requires us to look at our peers not as competitors on a bell curve, but as fellow human beings who are likely harboring the exact same insecurities and anxieties that we are. If we can find the strength to drop the masks and share our genuine struggles, we might just discover that we were never really alone in the first place.
